title: On certain C0-aspects of contactomorphism groups
speaker: Vukašin Stojisavljević
affiliation: Centre de recherches mathématiques, University of Montreal
abstract: We will explore certain C0-rigidity and flexibility phenomena in the study of contact transformations. In particular, we will show how the dichotomy between contact squeezing and non-squeezing is reflected in the group of contact homeomorphisms. We will also define a new conjugation-invariant norm on the group of smooth contactomorphisms and study its relation to the contact fragmentation norm. The main technical ingredient is a result stating that Sandons spectral norm is C0-locally bounded. The talk is based on a joint work with Baptiste Serraille.

proof_stages.label: Contact squeezing versus nonsqueezing background
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up the contrast between symplectic nonsqueezing, complete squeezing in standard contact Euclidean space, and contact nonsqueezing on the S1-periodic contact model, with contact Hamiltonians recalled as the source of contact isotopies.

proof_stages.label: Rokhlin property as the group-theoretic test
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines the Rokhlin property for a topological group, explains that continuous conjugacy invariants obstruct it, and states the main dichotomy: the C0 contact homeomorphism group in the squeezable model has the Rokhlin property while the nonsqueezing S1 model does not.

proof_stages.label: Non-Rokhlin proof from nonsqueezing
proof_stages.summary: Assuming a dense conjugacy class in the nonsqueezing model, the support identity supp(psi phi psi^{-1})=psi(supp phi) and a Reeb-rotation element force an inverse conjugator to squeeze a larger domain into a smaller one, contradicting contact nonsqueezing.

proof_stages.label: Rokhlin proof by packing squeezed conjugates
proof_stages.summary: For the fully squeezable contact Euclidean model, a countable dense set of contact homeomorphisms is squeezed into disjoint shrinking balls and assembled into one C0-convergent map whose conjugacy class closure contains every dense-set element and hence the whole group.

proof_stages.label: Sandon's integer-valued spectral norm
proof_stages.summary: After noting a no-go theorem for genuinely continuous conjugation-invariant contact norms, the speaker introduces Sandon's unbounded integer-valued conjugation-invariant norm gamma, built from generating-function spectral invariants, translated points, and integer parts of their actions.

proof_stages.label: C0 local boundedness and lower semicontinuity of gamma
proof_stages.summary: The main C0 theorem for gamma is that sufficiently C0-small contactomorphisms have gamma at most 2, more precisely their translated-point spectrum lies in a half-length action interval, which yields local boundedness, lower semicontinuity, and an extension to contact homeomorphisms.

proof_stages.label: Applications to conjugacy closures and fragmentation
proof_stages.summary: The C0 properties of gamma are used to bound possible intersections of conjugacy-class closures, reprove non-Rokhlin in the nonsqueezing model more strongly, and compare gamma with an abstract conjugation word norm to deduce unboundedness of the contact fragmentation norm.

examples.name: General linear group and eigenvalues
examples.note: The general linear group is used to show concretely how a continuous conjugacy invariant, here the spectrum or characteristic polynomial, prevents a dense conjugacy class.
examples.engagement_reason: he checks the general linear group against the Rokhlin property by using eigenvalues/characteristic data as continuous conjugation invariants

examples.name: Volume-preserving compactly supported homeomorphisms
examples.note: The group of compactly supported volume-preserving homeomorphisms of Euclidean space illustrates the same obstruction principle, with volume of the support playing the role of the continuous conjugacy invariant.
examples.engagement_reason: he verifies non-Rokhlin behavior for volume-preserving compactly supported homeomorphisms via preservation of the volume of the support

passes.reason: This is an ordinary transformation group, and preservation of support volume is used to rule out the Rokhlin property.
